thread.title: Biden, bureaucrats scramble to set roadblocks to Trump-proof federal government
thread.title_full: Biden, bureaucrats scramble to set roadblocks to Trump-proof federal government

author: Jeff Mordock
title: Biden, bureaucrats scramble to set roadblocks to Trump-proof federal government
text:
Democrats are racing against time to set lawful "land mines" to blunt the impact of President-elect Donald Trump, scrambling to rush billions in cash out the door, confirm judges and entrench climate and health policies before the incoming administration can dismantle all of it. Democrats are racing against time to set bureaucratic "land mines" to blunt the impact of President-elect Donald Trump, scrambling to rush billions of dollars out the door, confirm judges and entrench climate and health policies before the incoming administration can dismantle all of it.
With less than two months until the president-elect takes over, Democrats are doing what they can to "Trump-proof" the government.
The White House is doling out billions in infrastructure and manufacturing spending, along with aid to Ukraine. Agencies are furiously issuing rules, and Senate Democrats are trying to expedite confirming Biden-appointed judges, gearing up for upcoming legal fights with the Trump administration.
And federal workers are erecting procedural barricades, trying to unionize and lining up lawyers to stave off mass firings promised by Mr. Trump.
Come Jan. 20, Mr. Trump will control the purse strings and be able to undo much of what Mr. Biden has done through executive action. Even with Mr. Trump in charge, however, things in Washington don't change on a dime. Experts say Democrats' flurry of moves will create plenty of headaches for Mr. Trump when he strides into office.
"It matters or else they wouldn't be doing it. It comes to down setting land mines and making it more difficult for the incoming administration to reverse those changes," said Tad DeHaven, a policy analyst with the libertarian Cato Institute.
SEE ALSO: Americans warming to Trump; Pew survey says voters shifting to GOP, away from Democrats
Spending
Congress approved four major bills under Mr. Biden's watch that created roughly $4.8 trillion in new spending. The laws include the $1.9 trillion stimulus package; a $1.2 trillion infrastructure law to improve the nation's roads and bridges; a $1.2 trillion health, climate and tax law; and a $52 billion bill to boost semiconductor manufacturing in the U.S.
The administration doled out grants for about 98% of the funding for the four bills…
Jeff Mordock
